cc:cc-313.fact: Outside a Karratha pub, DAVIS shoves WALSH in the chest with both hands after an argument. WALSH is not injured and DAVIS has no lawful excuse. The shove is an unlawful application of force — common assault.

els.t: Any person who unlawfully assaults another is guilty of a simple offence and is liable —

cc:cc-317.fact: BAXTER punches LEE once in the face during a dispute over a parking bay. LEE's lip splits and bleeds — an injury that interferes with his comfort: bodily harm. The assault caused that harm.

cc:cc-318.fact: While being lawfully arrested, GRANT spits in the face of the arresting officer. The officer is a public officer performing a function of his office — the assault is a serious assault under the aggravated categories in subsection (1).

els.t: Any person who — [(a)-(c) deleted]

cc:cc-318.pen: Summary conviction penalty: in a case to which subsection (1)(m) applies: imprisonment for 3 years and a fine of $36 000. [(1A)      deleted]

cc:cc-297.fact: In a bar, HOLT smashes a glass into REID's head. REID suffers a fractured eye socket requiring surgery — an injury endangering, or likely to endanger, health: grievous bodily harm.

cc:cc-279.fact: After threatening to kill him earlier that day, CROSS stabs MARSH twice in the chest with a kitchen knife. MARSH dies. The intent to cause death (or at least an injury likely to endanger life) makes the unlawful killing murder.

els.t: If a person unlawfully kills another person and —

cc:cc-280.fact: WEBB punches WALKER once in a sudden argument. WALKER falls, hits his head on the kerb and later dies. If the killing is unlawful but the elements of murder under s 279 are not made out, WEBB commits manslaughter.

cc:cc-281.fact: NASH strikes DEAN with a single unprovoked punch to the head outside a nightclub; DEAN dies. Even if death was not foreseeable, the assault caused the death — unlawful assault causing death.

els.t: If a person unlawfully assaults another who dies as a direct or indirect result of the assault, the person is guilty of a crime and is liable to imprisonment for 20 years.

cc:cc-300.fact: Over six months, KANE assaults his partner in February, threatens to kill her in April (s 338B), and damages her car door in June (s 444) — each an act of family violence against the same person. Three or more such acts over the period can constitute persistent family violence.

els.t: A person commits a crime if the person persistently engages in family violence.
A charge of an offence under subsection (1) —

cc:cc-300.pen: Penalty for this subsection: imprisonment for 14 years.

cc:cc-338B.fact: FLYNN sends his ex-housemate a message: 'I will kill you when I find you.' A threat to kill made by any means — the offence is complete on the making of the threat, aggravating circumstances aside.

els.t: where the threat is to kill a person —
in the case of any other threat —

cc:cc-338E.fact: Over three weeks SHARP repeatedly waits outside PRICE's gym, follows her bus home and sends dozens of unwanted messages, intending to intimidate her. The repeated pursuit-type conduct with that intent is stalking.

els.t: A person who pursues another person with intent to intimidate that person or a third person, is guilty of a crime and is liable —

cc:cc-332.fact: Two men force COLE into a van, drive him to a unit and message his family demanding $50,000 for his release. Detaining him with intent to gain a benefit — the demand — is kidnapping.

els.t: For the purposes of this section and section 333, a person who deprives another person of personal liberty —

cc:cc-333.fact: A security guard locks a suspected shoplifter in a back office for an hour with no lawful authority and against her will. Depriving another of personal liberty — deprivation of liberty.

cc:cc-371.fact: OWEN borrows his neighbour's mower for the weekend, then sells it at a swap meet and keeps the cash. Taking was innocent; the later fraudulent conversion to his own use with intent to permanently deprive engages the definition of stealing.

els.t: A person who fraudulently takes anything capable of being stolen, or fraudulently converts to his own use or to the use of any other person any property, is said to steal that thing or that property.
A person who takes anything capable of being stolen or converts any property is deemed to do so fraudulently if he does so with any of the following intents, that is to say —

cc:cc-378.fact: A casual worker takes $180 from the till over two shifts and spends it. The thing stolen is the employer's money — stealing, with the penalty (and aggravated forms) set by this section.

cc:cc-371A.fact: TATE takes a stranger's parked car without consent, drives it all weekend and dumps it. Even without intent to permanently deprive, unlawfully using the motor vehicle is treated as stealing under this section.

cc:cc-392.fact: Wearing a balaclava, REES presents a screwdriver at a service-station console operator and yells at her to empty the till. He takes $400 and runs. Stealing with a threat of violence immediately before the taking, in order to obtain the cash — robbery; the screwdriver engages the armed limb.

els.t: to obtain the thing stolen; or
to prevent or overcome resistance to its being stolen,

if immediately before or at or immediately after the commission of the offence the offender is armed with any dangerous or offensive weapon or instrument or pretends to be so armed, to imprisonment for life; or

cc:cc-401.fact: At 2 am, DRAKE climbs through an unlatched laundry window of a house intending to steal. Entering the place of another without consent with intent to commit an offence — burglary; a dwelling at night raises the aggravated form.

els.t: A person who enters or is in the place of another person, without that other person’s consent, with intent to commit an offence in that place is guilty of a crime and is liable —
A person who commits an offence in the place of another person, when in that place without that other person’s consent, is guilty of a crime and is liable —

cc:cc-409.fact: BLISS emails a business a bogus 'overdue' invoice with her own account details. The business pays $2,300. Obtaining a benefit by deceit with intent to defraud — fraud.

els.t: Any person who, with intent to defraud, by deceit or any fraudulent means —

cc:cc-414.fact: MOSS buys a new 85-inch TV for $150 cash from a stranger in a car park, boxed with security tags still attached. The circumstances support knowledge or belief that it was stolen when he received it — receiving.

cc:cc-444.fact: After a road-rage exchange, VOSS drags a key along both panels of QUINN's ute, causing $4,000 damage. Wilfully and unlawfully damaging the property of another — criminal damage.

els.t: Any person who wilfully and unlawfully destroys or damages any property is guilty of a crime and is liable —

cc:cc-248.fact: CAINE raises a bottle and steps at HUNT, who punches CAINE once and steps back. If HUNT believed the act was necessary to defend himself and the act was a reasonable response in the circumstances as he believed them, the harmful act is lawful self-defence.

els.t: A person’s harmful act is done in self-defence if —

cc:cc-23B.fact: At a crowded pub, a thrown dart ricochets off a beam and strikes a bystander. If the injury occurs by accident — an event not intended or foreseen and not reasonably foreseeable — criminal responsibility for it is excluded.

els.t: This section is subject to the provisions in Chapter XXVII and section 444A relating to negligent acts and omissions.
A person is not criminally responsible for an event which occurs by accident.

cc:cc-24.fact: At the airport carousel, LOWE takes a black suitcase honestly and reasonably believing it is his identical bag. The mistaken belief in that state of things means his responsibility is assessed as if the bag were his.

cc:cc-244.fact: A householder wakes to an intruder forcing the back door. Believing the intruder intends to commit an offence, the occupant uses force to prevent entry and detain him. This section sets out when that force is lawful — including that it must not be intended or likely to cause death unless the occupant fears violence.

els.t: It is lawful for a person (the occupant) who is in peaceable possession of a dwelling to use any force or do anything else that the occupant believes, on reasonable grounds, to be necessary —
A person is a home invader for the purposes of subsection (1) if the occupant believes, on reasonable grounds, that the person —

cc:cc-70A.fact: Asked three times by the owner to leave a bottle shop after abusing staff, GIBB stays put. Remaining on premises after being warned off by the occupier — trespass.

els.t: In this section — abattoir has the meaning given in the Animal Welfare Act 2002 section 36A;

cc:cc-70A.pen: Penalty for this subsection: imprisonment for 12 months and a fine of $12 000.

cc:cc-74A.fact: At a busy rank, INGRAM screams sustained abuse and challenges strangers to fight. Behaving in a disorderly manner in a public place — disorderly behaviour.

els.t: In this section — behave in a disorderly manner includes —

cia:s-128.fact: An officer finds PRYOR at 3 am in a commercial yard, gloves on, holding bolt cutters beside a cut padlock. The officer reasonably suspects PRYOR has committed burglary — an arrestable offence — and arrests him without a warrant under this power.

els.t: In this section — serious offence means an offence —

A police officer or a public officer may arrest a person for a serious offence if the officer reasonably suspects that the person has committed, is committing, or is just about to commit, the offence.

cia:s-39.fact: A patrol stops a ute matching a burglary lookout; in plain view are drills matching the stolen property list. This section's power to search a vehicle for things relevant to an offence applies.

els.t: If an officer reasonably suspects —

cia:s-41.fact: Investigating a drug supply operation, detectives prepare an application identifying the target unit and the things sought. This section governs applying for the search warrant; the issue and effect follow in ss 42–43.

els.t: Only a police officer or a public officer may apply for a search warrant.

cia:s-63.fact: Before placing an arrested person in the van, an officer runs hands over his outer clothing and empties his pockets. That is a basic search as defined — contrast the strip search definition in s 64.

cia:s-118.fact: In a murder investigation, a suspect allegedly confesses in the car on the way to the station, but the admission is not audiovisually recorded and no recording exception applies. This section makes such an admission inadmissible in a serious-offence case.

els.t: In this section — admission means an admission made by a suspect to a police officer or a CCC officer, whether the admission is by spoken words or by acts or otherwise; adult means a person who has reached 18 years of age; child means a person who is under 18 years of age; reasonable excuse, for the absence of an audiovisual recording of an admission, includes —
This section applies in respect of a suspect who is —

cia:s-140.fact: MARTIN is arrested at 14:00 for aggravated burglary. From arrest, the detention period this section fixes governs how long he can be detained for investigation before a senior officer must extend or he must be charged, released or brought before a court.

els.t: In this section — arrested suspect has the meaning given to that term by section 139; senior officer means —
For the purposes of this section, the detention of an arrested suspect is justified if the detention —

mda:s-6.fact: A search of HALE's flat finds 12 gram baggies of methylamphetamine, digital scales, $2,150 in small notes and a tick list. Possession of the prohibited drug with intent to sell or supply — the indicia evidence the intent.

els.t: A person commits a crime if the person —

mda:s-11.fact: POOLE is found with 3.4 g of methylamphetamine — above the 2.0 g Schedule V quantity. This section presumes an intent to sell or supply, which POOLE bears the burden of rebutting.

mda:s-32a.fact: KERR is convicted of possessing 31 g of methylamphetamine with intent to sell or supply — over the 28.0 g Schedule VII quantity. On application, the court must declare him a drug trafficker, with the confiscation consequences that follow.

els.t: If a person is convicted of —

roa:s-61.fact: An FVRO restrains DALE from contacting the person protected. Within a fortnight he messages her twice and attends her workplace. Each act breaches the restraining order.

els.t: A person who is bound by an FVRO and who breaches that order commits an offence.
A person who is bound by an MRO and who breaches that order commits an offence.

roa:s-61.pen: Penalty for this subsection: a fine of $10 000 or imprisonment for 2 years, or both.

wa:s-6.fact: During a vehicle search, officers find knuckle dusters in the centre console. Knuckle dusters are a prohibited weapon prescribed by the regulations — carrying or possessing them, absent an exception, is an offence.

els.t: Except as provided in subsections (2) and (3) and section 10, a

wa:s-6.pen: Penalty: imprisonment for 3 years and a fine of $36 000.

cols.label: Schedule V
cols.desc: Presumption of intent to sell or supply
